summary: This advice column addresses four reader questions spanning health disclosure in dating, navigating a friend's accidental outing, parameter optimization in computing, and whether women are obligated to fight sexism in male-dominated fields. The author balances pragmatic advice with personal candor, often distinguishing between 'good advice' and what she would actually do. The most substantial response tackles workplace sexism, arguing there is no obligation to be a role model for a miserable position. She reframes competitive behavior as systemic rather than personal, advising women to stay internally focused on their own goals rather than getting distracted by external slights.
key_points: Private health information has no disclosure obligation on early dates, but serious relationships require transparency
Competitive and sexist behavior in academia and business is a product of systems built around status and money
There is no obligation for women to 'stand and fight' in hostile fields just to serve as role models
Taking workplace sexism personally is a distraction from pursuing your actual goals
The author distinguishes between 'good advice' (stay out of it) and what she'd actually do (tell her friend)
money_quotes: There is absolutely no obligation at all to stand and fight, by any woman or man, whatsoever.
It's a silly argument that one should role model for a position that's miserable. It's almost ludicrously bait and switch, in fact.
I exist as such a threat against other people (both men and women), that I recognize sexist pushback almost as a sport.
Don't be surprised that, as you get closer to the goal, people will be more threatened, not less, and they will embarrass themselves with bad behavior.
It's not about some vague obligation to society. It's about a very real obligation towards yourself, which you set.
Staying external is always a mistake, just look at the dieting industry.
As for brute force optimization, yes go ahead but remember to have a clean data set to test your parameters on, because you're surely overfitting.
key_insight: Women in hostile professional environments owe nothing to abstract societal obligations and should focus on their own goals rather than martyring themselves as reluctant role models.
